title: Why We Demand Perfect Machines Yet Tolerate Human Carnage | NOEMA
gist: A neurosurgeon argues that autonomous vehicle adoption is being slowed not by weak safety data but by a 'felt safety' gap, and that the AV industry should borrow healthcare's decades of experience managing patient perception, algorithm aversion, and transparent error disclosure to convert overwhelming safety evidence into public trust.

insights.claim: Society demands flawless performance from machines while accepting tens of thousands of human-caused traffic deaths as a routine cost of daily travel, a double standard that kills people by slowing adoption of safer autonomous technology.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: The core investment thesis for AVs is a real safety edge; the binding constraint on scaling is perception, not performance, so trust-building capability becomes the differentiator.
insights.quote: We demand machines that function flawlessly, while accepting human-caused deaths and injuries as the cost of our daily travel.
insights.locator: The Inflated Mirror

insights.claim: Illusory superiority, the near-universal belief that one is an above-average driver, is a cross-cultural cognitive bias that makes people benchmark new technology against an idealized version of themselves rather than actual human performance.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Because the comparison baseline is inflated, marginal safety data alone will not move adoption; messaging must reset the reference point.
insights.quote: Nearly every driver believes they are above average.
insights.locator: The Inflated Mirror

insights.claim: Being safe and feeling safe are distinct problems, and improving objective performance does not automatically improve public perception of it.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Companies that treat perception as a separate engineered discipline, not marketing, will out-adopt technically-equivalent rivals.
insights.quote: being safe and feeling safe are not the same
insights.locator: The Inflated Mirror

insights.claim: During a December San Francisco power outage that darkened traffic signals across a third of the city, Waymo vehicles correctly treated dead signals as four-way stops, but viral videos of a few cars frozen with hazard lights defined public perception despite no crashes or injuries.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: A single visible-but-harmless failure mode can trigger regulatory hearings and reputational damage disproportionate to actual risk, a tail liability for AV operators.
insights.quote: Waymo later reported that its vehicles navigated more than 7,000 dark intersections during the outage.
insights.locator: On a Saturday night, five days before Christmas

insights.claim: On an average U.S. day roughly 115 Americans die in crashes with human drivers, a toll that generates no news coverage, unlike a single non-fatal AV incident.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: The asymmetry in media salience between routine human carnage and novel-machine glitches structurally biases the political environment against AVs.
insights.quote: about 115 Americans died in crashes with human drivers. That did not make the news.
insights.locator: On the same day, if it was an average day

themes.theme: Waymo Safety Data and Incident Record

insights.claim: Across more than 220 million miles, Waymo vehicles have been involved in 94% fewer serious-injury-or-worse crashes than human drivers on the same roads, with pedestrian injuries down 93%, cyclist crashes down 84%, and intersection crashes down 96%.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: If these self-reported figures hold as deployment scales, the safety delta is large enough to be a durable competitive and regulatory asset.
insights.quote: Waymo’s vehicles have been involved in 94% fewer crashes that cause serious injury or worse than human drivers on the same roads.
insights.locator: But in the areas where these autonomous vehicles operate

insights.claim: The population-level health effect of driverless vehicles, if results hold at scale, could rival that of seat belts or the decline in smoking.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Frames AVs as a public-health intervention of historic magnitude, a narrative that could unlock government support and insurance realignment.
insights.quote: the population-level health effect could rival those of seat belts or the decline in smoking
insights.locator: But in the areas where these autonomous vehicles operate

insights.claim: Most alarming AV headlines stem from millions of driver-assistance vehicles that still require a human at the wheel, not from fully driverless systems, and conflating the two distorts risk perception.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Investors and regulators should distinguish L2 assistance from L4 driverless when assessing safety and liability, as they carry very different risk profiles.
insights.quote: Many of the scariest headlines come from the millions of vehicles with driver-assistance features that still require a human at the wheel.
insights.locator: Of course, not all autonomous vehicles are the same

insights.claim: Waymo is currently the only AV operator publishing per-mile, geographically-detailed crash data enabling fair comparison with human drivers, while competitors Tesla robotaxis in Austin/Texas and Amazon's Zoox in Las Vegas and San Francisco do not.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Waymo's data transparency is a strategic moat in the trust battle; rivals' opacity is a competitive and regulatory vulnerability.
insights.quote: The data here is from Waymo because, so far, they are the only ones that provide such detail.
insights.locator: Other companies have also gotten in on this market

insights.claim: Waymo's technical malfunctions persist, including a June fleet-wide software recall and highway suspension after cars entered closed construction zones, an April incident driving into high water in San Antonio, and an earlier car sinking into wet concrete it read as drivable road.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Recurring edge-case failures mean recall and downtime risk remains a real operating cost even for the safety leader.
insights.quote: This June, Waymo recalled the software on all its vehicles and suspended highway driving after several cars entered parts of highways that were closed for construction.
insights.locator: Technical malfunctions still happen

insights.claim: In all three driverless Waymo crashes causing two deaths and one serious injury, police reported the Waymo was not at fault, with causes including a 98 mph human driver, a police-chase suspect running a red light, and a hit-and-run driver killing a motorcyclist who had rear-ended the Waymo.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Fault attribution matters for liability exposure; a clean not-at-fault record supports the insurance-math case favoring AVs.
insights.quote: Driverless Waymos have been involved in crashes that resulted in two deaths and one injury that police reported as serious, and in all three cases, the driverless car was not at fault.
insights.locator: Driverless Waymos have been involved in crashes

themes.theme: Healthcare's Playbook for Perception and Experience

insights.claim: Healthcare spent 30 years learning that clinical quality and patient experience are correlated but distinct, and that assuming good outcomes alone would produce trust was wrong, with consequences appearing one patient at a time.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: AV firms can compress a 30-year learning curve by importing healthcare's experience-measurement discipline rather than rediscovering it slowly.
insights.quote: quality and experience are correlated but distinct
insights.locator: The Signs We Read

insights.claim: Bad care experiences causally worsen clinical outcomes, as patients who feel disrespected or unheard show measurably higher hospital readmission and ER-return rates and longer stays even after adjusting for diagnosis.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Perception is not cosmetic; it feeds back into real outcomes, implying felt-safety failures in AVs could depress the actual usage that delivers the safety benefit.
insights.quote: Positive clinical outcomes are less likely in patients with bad care experiences.
insights.locator: The quality of care was there

insights.claim: Natural-language analysis of patient comments found trust-killers were rarely clinical errors but semiotic signals like a used bandage on the floor or a bloodstain on a doctor's scrubs, harmless cues that reminded patients perfect safety does not exist.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Semiotic failure, where signals diverge from delivered safety, is a distinct design problem AV firms must engineer against, since visible cues drive trust more than statistics.
insights.quote: They were reminders that there is no such thing as perfect safety, but who wants that reminder when they are sick?
insights.locator: When Lee’s team ran natural-language processing

insights.claim: Cellphone videos of frozen Waymos during the blackout were functionally the AV equivalent of a bandage on the floor: a visible signal of imperfection that diverged from the underlying safe performance.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Managing the visible 'signals' an AV emits during edge cases may matter more for adoption than incremental improvements in actual driving.
insights.quote: cellphone cameras captured what was essentially the bandage on the floor and the bloodstained scrubs of a self-driving car
insights.locator: In the December blackout

themes.theme: The Adoption Arc of Safety Technology

insights.claim: Every major automotive safety technology follows the same arc of initial resistance, peak anxiety, then fading distrust until it feels obviously lifesaving, as seen with seatbelts opposed as government overreach and anti-lock brakes distrusted for altered pedal feedback.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: The pattern implies AV adoption is a predictable maturation curve, so patient capital that endures the peak-anxiety phase is rewarded as the cultural default flips.
insights.quote: Initial resistance leads to peak anxiety that eventually fades until the tech itself feels obviously lifesaving.
insights.locator: The Arc Of Adoption

insights.claim: The 1974 mandated seatbelt interlock, which blocked cars from starting unless front occupants were belted, worked technically but felt intrusive and glitchy enough that Congress outlawed it within a year, setting automotive safety adoption back years.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Ignoring how safety tech feels can trigger backlash that reverses mandates, a cautionary precedent for heavy-handed AV rollouts.
insights.quote: Ignoring how a technology felt, even in the pursuit of preventing physical harm, set the adoption of automotive safety technology back years
insights.locator: The Arc Of Adoption

insights.claim: First-generation airbags deployed at consistently high force and killed some occupants, especially children and small adults, but the remedy was iterative redesign with size-and-position sensing by 1998 rather than removal, showing fears were right about danger but wrong about magnitude.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: The airbag path suggests AV fears may be directionally valid but overweighted, and iterative fixes preserve the net-lifesaving technology.
insights.quote: Our fears were right about the danger but wrong about its magnitude.
insights.locator: With airbags, fears weren’t entirely imagined

insights.claim: Direct exposure closes the fear gap: most Americans say they would be uncomfortable with AI-assisted diagnosis yet patients who receive AI-assisted screening report over 90% satisfaction, and confidence in self-driving cars runs far higher among riders than non-riders.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Ridership itself is the most effective trust-builder, favoring strategies that maximize first-time exposure over pure advertising.
insights.quote: Confidence in the technology runs far higher among riders than non-riders.
insights.locator: Eventually, exposure closes the fear gap

insights.claim: New York's Vision Zero intersection redesigns from 2014 initially increased total crashes and injuries while reducing fatalities, and Oslo and Helsinki achieved years with zero pedestrian and cyclist deaths through slow street overhauls and speed-limit cuts defended across multiple governments.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Safety gains often require accepting worse-looking short-term metrics; the AV industry lacks the political cover these cities had to buy that time.
insights.quote: total crashes and injuries increased, but fatalities declined, making the streets dramatically safer
insights.locator: New York City’s Vision Zero program

insights.claim: The AV industry has no credible authority positioned to tell the public that an awkward-looking incident like the San Francisco blackout was objectively safe, making the problem one of interpretation rather than performance.
insights.speaker: Trent Victor
insights.so_what: A trusted interpreter role is a missing institutional layer whose absence amplifies every visible glitch into a crisis.
insights.quote: isn’t a performance problem; it’s an interpretation problem
insights.locator: The autonomous vehicle industry doesn’t have that kind of political clout yet

themes.theme: Algorithm Aversion and the Blame Problem

insights.claim: Empathy is the cognitive tool humans use to forgive human error, but machine error lacks a single blameworthy agent and leaves people with an opaque, hard-to-accept version of fate.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: The absence of a forgivable agent is a structural trust deficit for AVs that no safety statistic resolves; assigning interpretable accountability matters.
insights.quote: Forgiveness needs an agent that perhaps could have done something differently and can be held responsible.
insights.locator: No One To Forgive

insights.claim: Behavioral scientists' algorithm aversion means people lose confidence in a machine faster than in a human after identical mistakes and will prefer a worse human over a better algorithm once the algorithm errs.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: A single visible AV error is punished disproportionately versus human error, so error-response and explanation design is as important as raw accuracy.
insights.quote: We prefer a worse human over a better algorithm if the latter has made an error.
insights.locator: People tend to lose confidence in a machine

themes.theme: The Missing Denominator and Reporting Reform

insights.claim: Federal AV crash reporting is a numerator without a denominator: operators must report crashes but not the miles driven or where, making meaningful comparison and accountability impossible.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Mandated per-mile, geolocated reporting verified against police and insurance records would reshape the competitive field and reward transparent operators like Waymo.
insights.quote: It’s a numerator with no denominator.
insights.locator: The Missing Denominator

insights.claim: The U.S. government's own annual rankings of what kills Americans do not list motor-vehicle deaths as a cause, burying tens of thousands of deaths inside a broader unintentional-injuries category and normalizing the toll.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Statistical invisibility of road deaths weakens the policy urgency that would otherwise accelerate AV adoption.
insights.quote: On its list of what kills Americans annually, motor-vehicle deaths are not listed as a cause.
insights.locator: Until this happens, we are flying blind

insights.claim: Medicare's path offers a template: it began publicly reporting standardized patient-experience surveys in 2008 and tied hospital payment to those scores by 2012, spawning an entire accountability profession.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Payment-linked public reporting is a proven lever; a parallel felt-safety metric tied to AV licensing could institutionalize trust.
insights.quote: By 2012, hospital performance on those surveys was tied to payment levels.
insights.locator: In 2008, Medicare began publicly reporting

insights.claim: The AV industry should make felt safety its obsession, measuring and publicly reporting it the same way crashes are counted.
insights.speaker: Thomas Lee
insights.so_what: Treating perception as a first-class, measured KPI is the proposed strategic pivot for the industry's next phase.
insights.quote: its obsession
insights.locator: Lee believes the autonomous vehicle industry

insights.claim: How AV companies respond after their product hurts someone is decisive, as shown when Cruise, a GM subsidiary, dragged a pedestrian about 20 feet after a hit-and-run threw her into its path and then omitted the dragging from its federal report, drawing a DOJ obstruction charge.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Post-incident dishonesty, not the initial not-at-fault collision, destroyed Cruise; disclosure integrity is an existential risk factor for AV operators.
insights.quote: The initial collision was not the company’s fault. What came after was.
insights.locator: What Honesty Saves

insights.claim: Cruise's mishandling ended the company: a $500,000 criminal fine, three years of federal oversight, suspended California permits, resigned leadership including the CEO, and GM winding the business down.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that reputational and regulatory failure can zero out an AV venture regardless of underlying technology.
insights.quote: Terms of the agreement included a $500,000 criminal fine, a mandated compliance program and three years of federal oversight.
insights.locator: Cruise eventually admitted responsibility

insights.claim: The University of Michigan health system's 2001 shift to disclosing its own errors, apologizing, and offering compensation reduced new malpractice claims and lawsuits and shortened resolution times, contrary to expectations of a lawsuit flood.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Radical transparency counterintuitively lowers liability, a proven playbook AV firms could adopt to defuse post-incident litigation risk.
insights.quote: Disclosure did not enlarge liability.
insights.locator: In 2001, the University of Michigan’s health system

insights.claim: Self-driving vehicles will displace some of the millions of Americans who drive for a living, with losses landing earliest and hardest on older, lower-paid, most-vulnerable workers, an issue safety data cannot answer because it is a real harm, not a misperception.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: Workforce displacement is a genuine, non-perceptual source of opposition requiring deliberate planning; it is already among the loudest sources of resistance in several cities.
insights.quote: this is not the kind of issue safety data can answer, because it isn’t a misperception
insights.locator: Being honest with the public

insights.claim: AI-assisted radiology reads have become common enough that unassisted reads are growing rare for some conditions, making AI use the standard of care and signaling a similar inflection point has arrived for transportation.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Slotkin
insights.so_what: As in medicine, AV use may cross into default standard-of-care status, eventually making human driving the choice one has to justify.
insights.quote: Driving will increasingly become a choice — and one you’ll have to explain.
insights.locator: Medicine has already faced what the driverless car industry
